title: Visualise the sleep space
subtitle: A five-minute pre-sleep visualisation for settling the mind into the room before it tries to settle into sleep.
excerpt: A five-minute pre-sleep visualisation. Rather than trying to fall asleep, this practice settles the mind into the specific room, the weight of the duvet, the sounds outside, the temperature of the air. Five minutes of attending to the room before the room tries to become sleep. Use when the mind is active and sleep feels like a goal to chase.
sourceNotes: Grounded attention to the sleep environment as a pre-sleep practice is documented across sleep-hygiene research and mindfulness traditions. The specific room-attending form used here is adapted from SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025), Day 1.

content.text: A five-minute pre-sleep visualisation for settling the mind into the room before it tries to settle into sleep. The practice doesn't pursue sleep. It attends to the specific room: the weight of the duvet, the sounds from outside, the temperature of the air. When the mind is busy attending to the room, it isn't busy pursuing the night. Use it lying down, in the first five minutes after the light goes out.
New to visualisation? Read
how visualisations work
first.
The practice
Settle in
Lights off. Lie in whatever position you'll sleep in. Eyes closed. One slow breath in, one slow breath out. Don't try to sleep yet. Just be in the room.
Attend to the room
Notice the weight of the duvet. Where it lands on the body, the chest, the feet, the shoulders. Is it even? Heavier on one side than the other?
Notice the sounds. The building settling, the traffic from outside, the silence between sounds. Don't name them as distractions, name them as the texture of the room. The room sounds like this.
Notice the temperature of the air on the face. The patch of the pillow that is cool, the patch that has warmed from the head. The dark behind the eyelids, whether it is uniform or has texture.

Move slowly through these details. Attend to each for a slow breath before moving to the next. You are mapping the room with attention. Sleep will come in the background; the attention is on the room.
When the mind wanders

When a thought arrives: a plan, a worry, a replay of the day, notice it and return to the room. Not by pushing the thought away, but by choosing the room: the duvet weight, the temperature, the dark. The return is the practice. One return, ten returns, it doesn't matter. Each one counts.
When this isn't working
If the mind is too active to attend to the room, do a tapping round first.
Tapping to let the day go
releases the day's residue before you try to settle. Then return to the room.
Where this practice comes from

Calm body-attention to the sleep space as a pre-sleep practice is well known in sleep research and mindfulness work. The room-attending form used here is adapted from SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025). The book uses attention work to help the body settle before sleep.
